title: Counted Macros
subtitle: {key:100}{key:@a} — run a macro a hundred times. Or until it errors out.
summary: Counts work on macros. 100@a runs macro a up to 100 times; if any iteration aborts (search miss, end of file), playback stops cleanly.

blocks.text: A macro that does its work and moves to the next item is a loop. {key:{n}}{key:@}{key:a} runs that loop n times — but it *stops as soon as the macro errors*. So you can confidently say "do this 1000 times" knowing it'll stop when there's nothing left.

blocks.label: Run macro a up to 100 times

blocks.title: Why Errors Are Helpful Here
blocks.text:
Vim's macro playback halts on an error. Search misses, edge-of-buffer motions, failed operations — anything that beeps interactively also aborts a macro mid-stream.

This is a *feature*. If your macro processes a list and you don't know how long it is, write the macro to fail gracefully when it runs out of work. Then run it with a count larger than you'll ever need:

  • Macro that searches forward for `TODO`, edits, moves to next: {key:1}{key:0}{key:0}{key:0}{key:@}{key:a}. When `/TODO` finds no more matches, the macro aborts cleanly.
  • Macro that processes a CSV row by jumping to columns: {key:1}{key:0}{key:0}{key:@}{key:a}. When the cursor falls off the end of the file, the next motion errors and playback stops.

Big counts don't cost much — Vim doesn't run the macro 1000 times unless there's work for it to do.

If you'd rather *suppress* errors and keep playing, use {key:1}{key:0}{key:0}{key:@}{key:a} inside an {key::}{key:s}{key:i}{key:l}{key:e}{key:n}{key:t}{key:!} or wrap with `try/catch` in scripting. For interactive work, the abort-on-error behavior is what you want.

blocks.title: Counts re-invoke macros, they don't transform them
blocks.text: {key:5}{key:@}{key:a} runs the recorded stream five times in sequence; it does not multiply counts inside the recording. Side effects (cursor position, register state, search state) carry over from one invocation to the next. That's how `5@a` plus a motion-at-end-of-recording produces a sweep instead of doing the same thing five times in place.
